games.explanation: Bassitt is a perfectly fine pitcher unless you're a left-handed hitter, in which case he's a buffet line, and the Athletics happen to roll out a lineup full of dangerous lefty bats who do their best work against righties. Kurtz in particular has been a different human against right-handed pitching, posting a .423 xwOBA that turns the top of this order into a real problem. The wind blowing in at 17 mph trims the homer variance, but it doesn't help a contact-prone arm who gives up a 38% hard-hit rate to lefties, and the A's are the better team here by a comfortable Elo margin anyway. Getting a coin-flip price on the side our model likes near 57% is the entire ballgame.

Soriano is the kind of arm that looks fine in a box score and falls apart the moment a real lineup makes him throw strikes, which is exactly what Toronto does to right-handers who can't find the zone. He walks nearly 11% of the hitters he faces, and Springer and Guerrero are the wrong two names to be nibbling against with the count leaning hitter. The Jays are also the better team here by a meaningful Elo gap, on the road or not, and the simulated score is a one-run game the market has tilted too far toward the home side. At -105 you're getting a coin-flip price on the side that wins this matchup closer to 62% of the time.

Backing the Cubs at plus money against deGrom feels wrong until you remember the market still prices him like it's 2019, not a guy whose contact quality has quietly slipped into the danger zone. Lefties have barreled him at over 11% this year, and the Cubs happen to roll out Happ and a top of the order built to do exactly that kind of damage. Taillon isn't deGrom, but he doesn't need to be in a closed-roof game where Chicago is the better team by a comfortable margin. Getting +110 on the side our numbers make a near 60% favorite is the kind of price that doesn't survive the afternoon.
